Title: Interior Toxins
Description: The materials used to make your home are just as important as the materials you use for items that you put into your home. If you put all your efforts into building a comfortable earth-sheltered home, you will introduce toxins into your living environment if you use traditional paints, synthetic rugs, and so forth. Research toxic items that are in a typical household.

Title: Toxic Item Analysis
Description: Use a scrap piece of paper to answer the following questions with your partner: -Of the items you researched, which one or ones do you think you could change all by yourself in your own home? -Of the items you researched, which one or ones do you think you could change with the help of others in your own home? -Of the items you researched, which one or ones do you think would be the best to change in your school? -Once finished, make sure both of your names are on the paper and place it in one of your portfolios.

Title: Holiday Footprint
Description: For many Americans, the Christmas holiday wouldn't seem the same without a tree, lights, lots of wrapped presents, and the smell of goodies and a holiday feast. Have you ever considered the amount of energy used during the holiday season? Use the data shown to find the total number of pounds of carbon dioxide produced per person during the holiday season. Now that you have figured out how much CO2 is used per American each Christmas, work with your partner to answer the following two questions. • Using what you know about holiday celebrations, list two or three things that take energy that you would add to the table. For example, some families put up outdoor lights and decorations. Estimate how much you think each additional item would add to the holiday carbon footprint. • Using what you know about holiday celebrations, list two or three things that could be adjusted to use less energy. For example, families could spend time together reading classic Christmas stories rather than watching television and movie specials.

Title: Hydrogen Production
Description: Electrolysis is one of the more common methods in producing hydrogen for use in fuel cells. To extract 1 m³ of hydrogen it requires 4.8 kWh (kilowatt-hours) of electricity. Assuming the cost of electricity is $0.07 per kWh, how much does it cost to produce 2 m³ of hydrogen?

Title: Nuclear Fuel Pellets
Description: In a pressurized water reactor, two pellets of nuclear fuel are the equivalent of 2 1/2 tons of coal. A coal mine in Colorado can produce 14 million tons of coal annually. How many nuclear fuel pellets are needed to equal this amount of coal? Assume that 2 1/2 tons of coal produces all the electricity a single person uses annually. How many years will 100 nuclear fuel pellets provide enough electricity for a family of four? Hint: 2 nuclear fuel pellets = 2 1/2 tons of coal.

Title: Cancer
Description: Cancer is a disease in which certain cells of the body lose the ability to control mitosis. Cancerous cells reproduce much more rapidly than normal. They lose their normal shapes and functions and form tumors that take over the affected organ and use the oxygen and nutrients that the normal cells need. In your Module library, research causes of cancer and ways to treat and prevent it. Write a short report giving the results of your research. Use complete sentences and correct grammar, spelling, and punctuation.

Title: Cell Composition
Description: The composition of a typical cell is shown in the graph displayed. An egg yolk is a single cell. Assume it has a typical composition. Find the amount of each material in a 63-gram egg yolk.

Title: Cell Shape
Description: Many cells have spherical shapes. The volume of a sphere is equal to 4/3 • pi • the cube of the radius. The formula is written mathematically as V = 4/3 • pi • r³. The number "pi" is equal to about 3.14. The cube of the radius is equal to the radius • the radius • the radius.

Title: Life Span
Description: In mammals, the average life span for most cells is between 25 and 70 hours. Mitosis in these cells lasts about an hour. Suppose a cell has a life span of 35 hours. a. If the cell enters mitosis every 6 hours, how many times will it divide in its lifetime? b. If every generation of this cell has the same life span and the same time between periods of mitosis, how many cells would the original cell produce in its 35 hours of life?

Title: STEM Cells
Description: Stem cells are "baby" cells – they still have the potential to develop into any kind of body cell. Because of this potential, stem cells may be able to provide cures for many devastating diseases, including diabetes, Parkinson's disease, and Alzheimer's disease. But to find these cures, scientists must do many years of research on stem cells. Stem cell research is a bioethical problem because most stem cells used in research come from human embryos. After four or five days, the fertilized egg has become a ball of many cells, or blastocyst, which is about the size of the dot on an i. Scientists can harvest the cells, grow them in the laboratory, and treat them so that they can develop into any type of cell. But this process destroys the developing embryo. Some people feel that it is very important to do embryonic stem cell research because it will likely result in cures for serious diseases that kill many people each year and devastate the lives of many more. Others feel that using human embryos in this manner is murder and should not be allowed, no matter what can be gained from it. Which side do you agree with and why? Consider the following issues: a. Should the sanctity of human life be considered in this decision? If so, which lives? b. What are the costs and benefits of the research? c. What are the benefits and dangers to patients? d. What are some potential uses of the research? e. Should we "play God;" that is, should we tinker with systems we don't understand? f. Are other methods available for treating these diseases? g. Are there possible compromises?

Title: Using the Microscope
Description: With your teacher's permission, you may also photograph, save, and print the microscopic views. You will need the microscope, lens paper, box of slides, pencil and notebook paper, and your Student Module notebook. To review how to focus the microscope, consult the page titled "How to Use the Microscope" in your student Module notebook. Choose one of the following activities: a. Focus on the Onion Mitosis slide. Locate, photograph, and label each stage of mitosis. Save the labeled stages in your class-hour folder, naming each photo by its stage and your initials. b. On the Human Muscle Types slide, look at all three types of muscle tissue. Using the resources in the Module library, identify each muscle type. Photograph, label, and save a photo of each type in your class-hour folder. On a piece of notebook paper, describe differences in the three types.
